title: Fantasy Tavern Encounters
description: The common room as a social ecosystem — the travelers, the regulars, the staff, the rumors, and the particular atmosphere of a place where everyone has a reason for being there and some of them would prefer not to say what it is.

entries.result: A bard who is genuinely talented, knows it, and is performing a song that is clearly about someone in the room who has not yet realized it.
A pair of old veterans at a corner table reconstructing a battle they were both in, with the same outcome, from positions that were apparently on different sides.
A merchant who is trying to sell something at this table specifically because she ran out of buyers in two towns before this and her cargo is time-sensitive.
A message left at the bar for someone with a description that matches a party member, from a name they don't recognize, unopened.
A local lord traveling incognito, poorly, drinking alone, watched by two guards who are also incognito, also poorly.
A drinking contest between a halfling and a dwarf that everyone in the tavern has quietly bet on, the bets not yet disclosed.
A traveler who arrived three days ago and hasn't left, paying their tab daily, waiting for something they haven't named.
A young wizard who has had one drink, has decided this is enough drinks to practice an untested spell, and requires gentle discouragement.
A table of farmhands celebrating something at a volume that is enthusiastic rather than aggressive, inviting everyone near them to join.
A bounty hunter reading a notice board and marking certain notices with a small stylus, then removing the marked ones and pocketing them.
A cook from the kitchen who has come out to personally deliver a dish and to watch the person who ordered it take their first bite.
An argument between two regulars about a debt that is twelve years old, picking up exactly where it left off the last time they argued, which was last week.
A table of merchants who became uncomfortable when the party walked in and are now trying to look comfortable, badly.
A stray cat that has lived here for years, occupies a specific chair, and becomes agitated only when certain kinds of people sit near it.
A cleric who has come here specifically to find the kind of person who frequents taverns, for mission-related reasons they will explain at length if asked.
A tavernkeep who remembers every patron who has ever stayed here and will volunteer relevant cross-referenced information about recent guests if approached correctly.
A table of off-duty town watch playing cards with their weapons still on, which is technically against their own rules.
A bard collecting songs — not performing — buying rounds in exchange for travelers singing or recounting songs from wherever they've been.
A couple at a corner table who have been arguing for an hour and are about to resolve it in a way that affects people around them.
A halfling who claims to be a retired adventurer with a specific specific claim that is either entirely true or entirely false, with nothing in between.
A serving staff member who is clearly counting the hours until their shift ends and who will, in fact, be extremely helpful if asked anything that doesn't require them to do extra work.
A map pinned to the notice board that someone has annotated in a personal code, left for someone who hasn't come to collect it.
A traveling physician who has had a long day and is determined to drink exactly one drink and not become anyone's doctor tonight, a goal she is already failing.
A table of pilgrims who are technically not supposed to be in a tavern and are clearly enjoying not being supposed to.
A merchant's guard who is off-duty and attempting to make friends, unsuccessfully, because everything about their bearing says 'on duty.'
A refugee who arrived today, has all their possessions in a pack, and is asking everyone for news of a specific village the party may have been to.
A card sharp who is not cheating, technically, but who has a memory for cards that is not technically natural.
A tavernkeep's daughter who is listening to every conversation in the common room simultaneously and will know everything the party discusses at dinner.
A minstrel who is very good at music and very bad at reading the room, playing jaunty songs at a table of people who have just received terrible news.
A man in the corner who has been nursing the same drink for four hours and whose attention to the room is professional rather than casual.
A table of traders from a distant region whose customs are different enough that their normal behavior reads as suspicious to the locals.
A barfight in the early stages — two people standing, volume rising — that either requires intervention or will resolve itself, and correctly predicting which is important.
An elderly regular who has seen the party's type before, would like them to know it, and has useful opinions about the last group like them that came through.
A rumor making the rounds about something the party has direct knowledge of, which is wrong in every specific detail and right in overall implication.
A lockbox under the bar that the tavernkeep holds for certain regulars, which someone tonight has urgently need to access, and the tavernkeep is stalling.
A young person who has run away from somewhere and who needs either help getting further or to be convinced to go back, and reading which requires judgment.
A table that has been reserved and is therefore empty in a full room, with no indication who reserved it or when they're coming.
A guild representative who is here collecting dues from members who are also here and who don't want to be seen paying.
A barfight from outside that comes through the door still in progress, involving people who are guests of the tavern and people who are not.
A tavernkeep who serves everyone, charges fairly, says nothing, hears everything, and has strong opinions that he will share with the right people at the right time.
A party of adventurers who came back from somewhere they won't describe, drinking to forget it, who will eventually start talking to anyone who buys them a round.
A rat problem that is not actually a rat problem — the behavior is too organized, the scale too consistent — and that the tavernkeep is reluctant to acknowledge.
A pair of halflings running a shell game on three humans who are losing, which is expected, and on each other, which is more interesting.
A table of locals celebrating a birthday for someone who is clearly not having a good birthday, keeping that fact from ruining everyone else's time.
A sailor from a landlocked town who became a sailor, sailed for twenty years, and has returned to find no one believes the twenty years.
A message written on the underside of the party's table in chalk, placed there for someone who sits in this specific spot.
A traveling judge who is eating dinner and does not want to be recognized as a judge and who has recognized someone else in this room as someone currently before his court.
A local healer who comes in every evening for one drink and to hear what's going on in the region, and who knows more about the local situation than anyone.
An argument between a new bard and the established bard over the same performance slot, being adjudicated by the tavernkeep who is trying to be fair.
A spy who is watching a specific table and who has not noticed the party noticing them.
A drunkard who is not drunk, performing drunkenness for reasons that become clear if the party watches long enough.
A group of children using the tavern's yard for a game that has evolved rules more complex than anything played inside.
A very young traveler, first time away from home, who is doing everything correctly and who is terrified, and who could use a kind word or a good example.
A table of thieves conducting a meeting with enough plausible cover conversation that it would take sustained attention to recognize what they're actually discussing.
A person asking about the same thing the party is looking for, from a different angle, with different information, who may be an ally or competition or both.
A story that an old regular is telling, badly, that contains information the party needs, buried in embellishments the party has to endure to reach it.
A love letter being drafted and redrafted at a corner table by someone with ink-stained fingers and the particular paralysis of someone trying to say something exact.
A local who knows what the party is after, will trade that knowledge for help with something specific, and is entirely willing to negotiate terms.
A table of mercenaries between contracts who are bored, professional, and assessing the room with the specific attention of people whose next job might walk in any moment.
A man who leaves a sealed package at the bar, states a name, and is gone before the package is examined. The name belongs to someone in the party.
A tavernkeep who is covering for a guest and who is very bad at it and who will reveal everything to anyone who asks twice.
A widow traveling to settle an estate who has been waiting for a traveling companion going her direction and who will pay for the privilege.
A retired monster hunter who has come home to live quietly and who absolutely does not want to hear about whatever the party is hunting.
A table of scholars on a research trip who have gotten slightly too deep into the local wine and are arguing about something theoretical with great intensity.
A gnome tinkerer offering to repair equipment at the table, with a kit, at rates substantially below market, while clearly having specific interest in what the equipment has been used for.
A card reader set up near the fire who is not a charlatan and whose readings have been causing small, private crises at the tables where she reads.
A traveling noble's servant sent ahead to arrange accommodations who has discovered there are none, and is attempting to buy someone out of their room.
A table that was occupied when the party arrived, that has been empty for an hour, whose occupants left everything — packs, cloaks, food — behind.
A bard performing a ballad that is clearly about a living person who is in the room, unflattering, with enough detail to be actionable, and who clearly doesn't know their subject is present.
A local election being held in the tavern's back room, which the party can observe through the open door, being contested in a way that will have visible effects on the town.
A traveling merchant whose inventory is legitimate but whose prices are too good, and who has a reason for that they're not offering voluntarily.
A pair of monks who have been arguing theology for four hours and who will include the party in the debate whether the party wishes to be included or not.
A serving staff member who is pocketing small amounts from every table in a way that is detectable if anyone is paying attention, which the tavernkeep apparently isn't.
A group of locals who are waiting for someone to leave — specifically, one person who is waiting to leave once the group outside disperses — and both sides know it.
A traveler from the party's home region, or who knows the party's home region well, who has news from there that the party doesn't have.
A table of gamblers whose game has a third layer — the gambling on the game is covering a negotiation the game is covering — and who would prefer no spectators.
A new tavernkeep in an establishment run by the same family for four generations, who is clearly out of their depth and who knows it and who is doing fine anyway.
A local who survived something terrible and drinks here most nights because it's the closest they can get to comfortable and who tells the story once, perfectly, to anyone who earns it.
A man asking after a specific room — not a room at this inn, a room at a specific location in the area — with a description that makes it sound significant.
A table of rangers rotating off duty who are more relaxed than most people assume rangers can be and who will discuss conditions in the surrounding wilderness in significant detail.
A ghost that's been haunting this tavern for thirty years, that the regulars have named and incorporated into nightly routine, that still occasionally startles new guests.
A tavernkeep's book — the actual ledger — visible under the bar, with notes in the margins that are not financial records.
A traveling family, four of them, taking their first rest in three days, too tired to be careful about what they say, and carrying something that is clearly not family luggage.
A serving apprentice on their first week who has done everything wrong, gotten yelled at twice, and is going to be excellent at this job in six months.
A conversation at a nearby table that is entirely mundane, in a language the party half-knows, containing a phrase that is not mundane.
A pair of dwarven prospectors who have returned from somewhere they're being very specific about not naming, purchasing supplies for a second trip immediately.
A local lord's steward conducting interviews — over drinks, casually — for a position the party might be qualified for and might not want.
A table that fills, one person at a time, with people who don't appear to know each other, who order the same thing, who leave together.
A contest — darts, arm-wrestling, riddles — with a prize that the party would actually find useful, open to entrants, with a current favorite who can be beaten.
A traveler who has been robbed, is unhurt, is furious, and has a description of the robbers that is specific enough to act on.
A door to the cellar that staff avoid, that guests are steered away from, and that makes a sound at irregular intervals that no one has explained.
A message chalked on the tavern's exterior door that has been there since before the party arrived, that locals walk past without reading, that is addressed to the party by general description.
A table of pilgrims who have been waiting three days for their group to complete, and whose group has now, apparently, completed, and who are packing to leave immediately.
A retired spy who drinks here every night, who was once very good at their job, and who has not stopped working — only changed who they report to.
A traveling show — three performers — setting up in the tavern yard, whose act is better than a traveling show should be and whose members are performing for reasons beyond entertainment.
A patron who recognizes one party member from a context the party member would prefer not discussed, and who is either going to be discreet or is going to need to be convinced to be.
A letter left at the bar six months ago, uncollected, that the tavernkeep has been holding, addressed to someone who clearly isn't coming for it.
A table of travelers who all arrived by different routes and who compare notes, discovering that each of them was told to come here by different people.
A brawl that begins suddenly, peaks quickly, and is over before the watch arrives, leaving behind the specific silence of a room that has collectively decided it didn't see anything.

The common room empties over the course of an hour — one table at a time, no announced reason — until the party is the only guests left, with the tavernkeep polishing the bar and looking like they expected this.
